question: What is the Norwegian CTR reporting threshold for cash transactions?
ground_truth: Under the Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act (Hvitvaskingsloven) Section 10(1)(b)(1), customer due diligence measures are mandatory for transactions of NOK 100,000 or more. Multiple transactions by the same customer that appear linked are aggregated. The report must be submitted to Økokrim (the Norwegian FIU) under Section 26.
contexts: Section 10 of the Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act (Act of 1 June 2018 No. 23), as translated by Finanstilsynet (unofficial translation): 'Section 10. Duty to apply customer due diligence measures. (1) Obliged entities shall apply customer due diligence measures upon: [...] b. transactions for customers with whom the obliged entity does not have an established customer relationship, in an amount of no less than: 1. NOK 100,000; [...] (2) The threshold amounts in subsection (1), letter b), nos. 1, 3 and 4 shall be calculated on an aggregate basis for transactions carried out in several operations that appear to be linked. If the amount is not known when the transaction is carried out, customer due diligence measures shall be applied as soon as the obliged entity becomes aware that the threshold amount has been exceeded.'

question: What is the mandatory retention period for AML transaction records under Norwegian law?
ground_truth: Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act Section 30 requires obliged entities to record and retain information for five years after the customer relationship has ended or the transaction has been carried out. Personal data must be erased upon expiry of the five-year limit. The overall retention period for personal data shall not exceed ten years. This legal obligation overrides GDPR Article 5(1)(e) storage limitation under GDPR Article 6(1)(c).
contexts: Section 30 of the Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act (Act of 1 June 2018 No. 23), as translated by Finanstilsynet: 'Section 30. Recording and retention of information and documents. Erasure of personal data. (1) Obliged entities shall record and retain information and documents obtained and prepared in connection with measures under Sections 9 to 26 for five years after the customer relationship has been ended or the transaction has been carried out. Such retention shall take place in a diligent manner preventing unauthorised third-party access. This obligation shall not extend to recording or retention of any address registered as confidential or strictly confidential in the National Population Register. (2) The personal data shall be erased upon expiry of the five-year time limit. Other information may be retained for longer. These provisions do not curtail any obligation to retain or store information and documents under other regulatory provisions. (3) Obliged entities shall maintain systems enabling swift and complete replies to any requests from Økokrim, the supervisory authority or other government bodies as to whether the entity in question has, or over the course of the last five years has had, any customer relationship with specific persons and as to the nature of any such customer relationship. (4) The Ministry may in regulations lay down further rules on how information and documents shall be recorded and retained, as well as on retention of personal data for more than five years. The overall retention period for personal data shall not exceed ten years.'

GDPR Article 5(1)(e), Regulation (EU) 2016/679: personal data shall be 'kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89(1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (storage limitation)'. Note: the AML Act retention obligation constitutes a legal obligation under GDPR Article 6(1)(c) that overrides the general storage limitation principle.

question: What Enhanced Due Diligence requirements apply to Politically Exposed Persons?
ground_truth: Norwegian AML Act Section 18 requires obliged entities to apply enhanced customer due diligence for PEPs, including obtaining senior management approval before establishing or continuing the customer relationship, and establishing source of wealth and source of funds. Enhanced ongoing monitoring applies for at least one year after the person leaves the relevant position (Section 18(4)). FATF Recommendation 12 requires the same measures for foreign PEPs.
contexts: Section 18 of the Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act (Act of 1 June 2018 No. 23), as translated by Finanstilsynet: 'Section 18. Enhanced customer due diligence measures in relation to politically exposed persons and close family members of and persons known to be close associates of politically exposed persons. (1) Obliged entities shall apply enhanced customer due diligence measures when the customer, any person who acts on behalf of the customer or who has been granted a right of disposal over an account or a deposit, or any beneficial owner, is a politically exposed person. (2) When establishing a customer relationship with a politically exposed person or when an existing customer, any person who acts on behalf of the customer or who has been granted a right of disposal over an account or a deposit, or any beneficial owner, becomes a politically exposed person, obliged entities shall, inter alia, a. ensure that senior management approval is obtained before the establishment or continuation of the customer relationship; b. apply adequate measures to establish the source of wealth and the source of funds. [...] (4) Obliged entities shall apply the measures under subsections (1) to (3) for at least one year after the politically exposed person leaves the position or function, cf. Section 9. (5) Subsection (1) to (4) shall apply correspondingly to close family members of and persons known to be close associates of a politically exposed person.'

FATF Recommendation 12, Politically exposed persons (FATF Recommendations 2012, updated October 2025): 'Financial institutions should be required, in relation to foreign politically exposed persons (PEPs) (whether as customer or beneficial owner), in addition to performing normal customer due diligence measures, to: (a) have appropriate risk-management systems to determine whether the customer or the beneficial owner is a politically exposed person; (b) obtain senior management approval for establishing (or continuing, for existing customers) such business relationships; (c) take reasonable measures to establish the source of wealth and source of funds; and (d) conduct enhanced ongoing monitoring of the business relationship. [...] The requirements for all types of PEP should also apply to family members or close associates of such PEPs.'

question: What are the PSD2 SCA low-value exemption thresholds?
ground_truth: Under Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2018/389 (PSD2 RTS on SCA), Article 16 exempts remote electronic payment transactions from SCA where the amount does not exceed EUR 30, subject to cumulative limits: either the total of previous exempt transactions since last SCA does not exceed EUR 100, or the number of consecutive exempt transactions does not exceed 5. PSPs must apply SCA once either cumulative limit is reached.
contexts: Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2018/389 of 27 November 2017 supplementing Directive (EU) 2015/2366 (PSD2 RTS on SCA), Article 16 — Contactless payments at point of sale and Article 16 on transaction risk analysis: Article 16 low-value exemption: Payment service providers shall be allowed not to apply strong customer authentication where the payer initiates a remote electronic payment transaction provided that the following conditions are met: (a) the amount of the remote electronic payment transaction does not exceed EUR 30; and (b) either: (i) the cumulative amount of previous remote electronic payment transactions initiated by the payer since the last application of strong customer authentication does not exceed EUR 100; or (ii) the number of previous remote electronic payment transactions initiated by the payer since the last application of strong customer authentication does not exceed 5 consecutive individual remote electronic payment transactions. The EBA Q&A 2018_4429 clarifies: PSPs should decide at the outset which cumulative limit they use (monetary EUR 100 or count of 5 transactions) rather than applying different limits on a transaction-by-transaction basis. Limits under Article 16 are applied at the account holder level and calculated separately for each payment instrument.

Finanstilsynet: Norwegian implementation of PSD2 SCA requirements. Finanstilsynet supervises compliance with the Payment Services Act (betalingstjenesteloven) which implements Directive (EU) 2015/2366. Norwegian PSPs are subject to the same SCA requirements and exemption thresholds as elsewhere in the EEA. Finanstilsynet requires quarterly reporting of exemption usage rates and fraud rates per exemption type, aligned with the EBA Guidelines on major incidents reporting under PSD2.

question: What constitutes a suspicious transaction under Norwegian AML law that must be reported?
ground_truth: Norwegian AML Act Section 25 requires further examination when circumstances may indicate money laundering or terrorist financing, including transactions that appear to lack legitimate purpose, are unusually large or complex, or are made to persons in countries lacking satisfactory AML measures. Section 26 then requires submission to Økokrim where, after further examination, there remain grounds for suspicion. The obligation applies regardless of transaction amount. FATF Recommendation 20 establishes the same international standard.
contexts: Section 25 and 26 of the Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act (Act of 1 June 2018 No. 23), as translated by Finanstilsynet: 'Section 25. Duty to conduct examinations. (1) If obliged entities detect circumstances which may indicate that funds are associated with money laundering or terrorist financing, further examinations shall be conducted. (2) Further examinations shall always be conducted if circumstances are detected which are not consistent with the obliged entity's knowledge of the customer or the purpose and intended nature of the customer relationship, or if a transaction: a. appears to lack a legitimate purpose; b. is unusually large or complex; c. is unusual in view of the customer's known pattern of business or personal transactions; d. is made to or from a person in a country or area which does not have satisfactory measures to combat money laundering and terrorist financing; e. is otherwise of an unusual nature.' 'Section 26. Duty to report. Duty to disclose. Waiver of liability. (1) If, after further examinations, there are circumstances giving grounds for suspicion of money laundering or terrorist financing, obliged entities shall submit information to Økokrim [the FIU] on such circumstances. Obliged entities shall submit any other necessary information at the request of Økokrim, irrespective of whether the obliged entity has submitted information pursuant to the first sentence of its own volition. [...] (4) The submission of information to Økokrim in good faith does not constitute a violation of any duty of confidentiality and does not give rise to any liability for damages or any criminal liability, unless there is gross negligence.'

FATF Recommendation 20, Reporting of suspicious transactions (FATF Recommendations 2012, updated October 2025): 'If a financial institution suspects or has reasonable grounds to suspect that funds are the proceeds of a criminal activity, or are related to terrorist financing, it should be required, by law, to report promptly its suspicions to the financial intelligence unit (FIU).'

question: What is structuring and why is it illegal under Norwegian AML regulations?
ground_truth: Structuring (also known as smurfing) is deliberately breaking up transactions to avoid reporting thresholds, such as making multiple cash deposits just below NOK 100,000 to avoid Currency Transaction Report obligations. It is itself a money laundering predicate offence under Norwegian Penal Code Section 332 (referenced in Norwegian AML Act Section 2(a)). Norwegian AML Act Section 10(2) explicitly requires aggregation of linked transactions. FATF Recommendation 3 requires countries to criminalise money laundering including structuring.
contexts: Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act (Act of 1 June 2018 No. 23), Section 2(a) and Section 10(2), as translated by Finanstilsynet: 'Section 2. Definitions. The following definitions shall apply for purposes of this Act: a. money laundering: acts as described in Section 332 and Section 337 of the Penal Code.' 'Section 10(2): The threshold amounts in subsection (1), letter b), nos. 1, 3 and 4 shall be calculated on an aggregate basis for transactions carried out in several operations that appear to be linked. If the amount is not known when the transaction is carried out, customer due diligence measures shall be applied as soon as the obliged entity becomes aware that the threshold amount has been exceeded.' The NOK 100,000 CDD threshold in Section 10(1)(b)(1) must be calculated on an aggregate basis — this directly addresses structuring typologies where customers attempt to keep individual transactions below the threshold.

FATF Recommendation 3, Money laundering offence (FATF Recommendations 2012, updated October 2025): 'Countries should criminalise money laundering on the basis of the Vienna Convention and the Palermo Convention. Countries should apply the crime of money laundering to all serious offences, with a view to including the widest range of predicate offences.' The FATF Glossary defines 'smurfing' as a structuring method involving multiple individuals making sub-threshold transactions on behalf of a principal party — a typology explicitly covered under FATF Recommendation 3 and the interpretive note to Recommendation 1 (risk-based approach).

question: What are the FATF high-risk jurisdiction implications for Norwegian banks?
ground_truth: FATF Recommendation 19 requires financial institutions to apply enhanced due diligence measures to business relationships and transactions with persons from countries the FATF has identified as high-risk. Countermeasures are mandatory for FATF blacklisted jurisdictions (Public Statement) and EDD is required for grey-list (ICRG) countries. Finanstilsynet issues supervisory guidance aligned with FATF updates.
contexts: FATF Recommendation 19, Higher-risk countries (FATF Recommendations 2012, updated October 2025): 'Financial institutions should be required to apply enhanced due diligence measures to business relationships and transactions with natural and legal persons, and financial institutions, from countries for which this is called for by the FATF. The type of enhanced due diligence measures applied should be effective and proportionate to the risks. Countries should be able to apply appropriate countermeasures when called upon to do so by the FATF. Countries should also be able to apply countermeasures independently of any call by the FATF to do so. Such countermeasures should be effective and proportionate to the risks.'

Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act Section 25(2)(d): 'Further examinations shall always be conducted if circumstances are detected which are not consistent with the obliged entity's knowledge of the customer or the purpose and intended nature of the customer relationship, or if a transaction: [...] d. is made to or from a person in a country or area which does not have satisfactory measures to combat money laundering and terrorist financing.' Finanstilsynet publishes periodic supervisory circulars listing relevant FATF risk notices and applying them to Norwegian-licensed institutions. For FATF blacklisted jurisdictions, Norwegian banks must apply countermeasures including enhanced transaction monitoring, senior management approval for new relationships, and additional documentation requirements.

question: How does the EU AI Act affect our AML compliance AI systems?
ground_truth: Under EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) Article 6(2), AI systems listed in Annex III are considered high-risk. AI systems used for creditworthiness assessment or risk assessment are listed in Annex III point 5. AML compliance AI systems that profile individuals or assess transaction risk fall in this category. Article 9 requires a risk management system, Article 10 data governance, Article 13 transparency, Article 14 human oversight (no fully automated adverse decisions), and Article 17 a quality management system.
contexts: EU Artificial Intelligence Act, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of 13 June 2024, Article 6 — Classification Rules for High-Risk AI Systems: '2. In addition to the high-risk AI systems referred to in paragraph 1, AI systems referred to in Annex III shall be considered to be high-risk. [...] 3. By derogation from paragraph 2, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall not be considered to be high-risk where it does not pose a significant risk of harm to the health, safety or fundamental rights of natural persons, including by not materially influencing the outcome of decision making. [...] Notwithstanding the first subparagraph, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall always be considered to be high-risk where the AI system performs profiling of natural persons.' Annex III, Point 5 — Access to essential private and public services and benefits: AI systems intended to be used by obliged entities covered by Union law on preventing money laundering and terrorist financing for the purposes of individual risk assessment, and AI systems intended to be used for creditworthiness assessment or credit scoring, with the exception of AI systems used for the purpose of detecting financial fraud.

EU Artificial Intelligence Act Article 14 — Human Oversight: High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way, including with appropriate human-machine interface tools, that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which the AI system is in use. This requires that deployers ensure human oversight appropriate to the circumstances and the risks, that human overseers are able to intervene on or interrupt the system, and that the system includes the functionality to enable the persons responsible for their oversight to understand the system's capabilities and limitations.

question: What KYC refresh frequency is required for high-risk customers?
ground_truth: Norwegian AML Act Section 24 requires obliged entities to monitor customer relationships on an ongoing basis, applying customer due diligence measures on a regular basis and whenever there are doubts about information accuracy. Section 24(3) requires enhanced ongoing monitoring for PEPs. The frequency must be risk-based. FATF Recommendation 10 (interpretive note) specifies that ongoing monitoring frequency should reflect the risk level of the customer.
contexts: Section 24 of the Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act (Act of 1 June 2018 No. 23), as translated by Finanstilsynet: 'Section 24. Ongoing monitoring of customer relationships. (1) Obliged entities shall monitor customer relationships on an ongoing basis. The monitoring shall, inter alia, include monitoring that transactions carried out in the customer relationship are in accordance with the information obtained by the obliged entity on the customer, the business and risk profile of the customer, the source of funds and the purpose and intended nature of the customer relationship. (2) Obliged entities shall apply customer due diligence measures on a regular basis as part of their ongoing monitoring. Customer due diligence measures shall in any event be applied when there are doubts about the veracity or adequacy of previously obtained information. (3) If the customer or any beneficial owner is a politically exposed person or a close family member of or a person known to be a close associate of a politically exposed person, enhanced ongoing monitoring of the customer relationship shall be conducted. (4) If customer due diligence measures as part of ongoing monitoring cannot be applied, obliged entities shall discontinue the customer relationship.'

FATF Recommendation 10, Customer due diligence (FATF Recommendations 2012, updated October 2025), interpretive note: Financial institutions should be required to apply CDD measures on a risk-sensitive basis. For higher-risk categories of customer, business relationship or transaction, enhanced CDD measures should be applied. The ongoing monitoring requirement in Section 24 of the Norwegian AML Act implements FATF Recommendation 10's requirement for 'ongoing due diligence on the business relationship and scrutiny of transactions undertaken throughout the course of that relationship to ensure that the transactions being conducted are consistent with the institution's knowledge of the customer, their business and risk profile, including, where necessary, the source of funds.'

question: What is the Finanstilsynet reporting deadline for suspicious transaction reports under Norwegian AML law?
ground_truth: Norwegian AML Act Section 27 requires that suspicious transactions not be carried out until Økokrim has been informed. Section 26(1) requires submission of information to Økokrim (the Norwegian FIU, Enheten for finansiell etterretning — EFE) as soon as grounds for suspicion arise after further examination under Section 25. There is no fixed calendar deadline — the obligation is immediate upon the conclusion of examinations. Section 27(2) permits carrying out the transaction if suspension is impossible, but requires immediate notification to Økokrim in that case.
contexts: Section 26 and 27 of the Norwegian Anti-Money Laundering Act (Act of 1 June 2018 No. 23), as translated by Finanstilsynet: 'Section 26. Duty to report. Duty to disclose. Waiver of liability. (1) If, after further examinations, there are circumstances giving grounds for suspicion of money laundering or terrorist financing, obliged entities shall submit information to Økokrim [the FIU] on such circumstances.' 'Section 27. Carrying out suspicious transactions. (1) Obliged entities shall not carry out suspicious transactions until Økokrim has been informed. Økokrim may in special cases prohibit the carrying out of a transaction. (2) If it is impossible to suspend the transaction, or if suspending the transaction may impede enquiries in relation to any person who may benefit from a suspicious transaction, Økokrim shall be notified immediately after the transaction has been carried out.'

Finanstilsynet (Norwegian Financial Supervisory Authority) — AML Supervision: Finanstilsynet is the prudential supervisor for AML compliance in Norway. Supervised entities include banks, investment firms, insurance companies, and payment institutions. Finanstilsynet can order immediate corrective measures and impose administrative sanctions under Norwegian AML Act Chapter 9. The FIU function is performed by Økokrim's money laundering section (Enheten for finansiell etterretning — EFE). STR reports are submitted electronically through the goAML system. There is no fixed monthly cycle for STRs — each must be filed immediately upon the conclusion of suspicious activity examinations under Section 25.
